parameters.name: ISS scheduled maintenance crew time per crewmember-week (flight data 2017-2023)
parameters.source: Lynch et al., ICES-2024-132, Table 4 (OPTimIS dataset, Oct 2017-Dec 2023)
parameters.notes: Corrective + preventive scheduled maintenance. Adjacent categories: Routine Ops 4.5, Logistics 2.3 h/CM-wk. Use ~15% of a 40 h work-week as L0 maintenance fraction; add Mars comm-delay overhead.

parameters.name: ISS ECLSS-only maintenance and repair crew time, station-wide
parameters.source: Owens et al., AIAA 2017-5124, citing Bagdigian et al., ICES-2015-094; design documents predicted ~1 h/week
parameters.notes: Actual exceeded design estimate (~1 h/wk) by >10x; Russell & Klaus 2007 found +60.4% in analyzed categories. Anchor for regenerative-ECLSS maintenance burden per ~6-crew outpost; apply as pessimism multiplier on bottom-up estimates.

parameters.name: Fraction of maintenance crew time that is corrective (vs preventive/scheduled)
parameters.source: Estimate: based on Russell & Klaus 2007 ISS Increments 4-8 category breakdown (35.3% of ECLSS maintenance time on air/waste repair) and ISS ops practice
parameters.notes: No clean modern public split; ISS planning emphasizes preventive. Treat corrective portion as stochastic (scales with realized failures), preventive as deterministic clock.

parameters.name: Fraction of ECLSS ORUs whose flight failure rate exceeded pre-flight estimate
parameters.source: Jones, ICES-2019-14 (14 of 20 ECLSS ORUs with changed estimates were worse; MADS vs ~8 yr flight data)
parameters.notes: ECLSS/fluid-handling specific. Contrast with MADS-wide Bayesian updates (see fraction_madswide_mtbf_adjusted_down).

parameters.name: Fraction of all ISS Crit-1/2 components whose Bayesian-updated MTBF decreased
parameters.source: Stromgren et al., AIAA 2016-5307 (MADS updates through June 2016; ~3% ended at <=0.25x initial MTBF; ~85% improved, 14% of those >=10x)
parameters.notes: Use as station-wide prior; the small worse-tail dominates system risk (asymmetry). ECLSS classes should be biased toward the Jones result.

parameters.name: Worst-case observed flight-to-predicted failure rate multiplier (ECLSS ORUs)
parameters.source: Jones, ICES-2019-14 (four UPA ORUs at 7.5-22x; seven other ORUs at 1.9-6.3x)
parameters.notes: Tail anchor for the epistemic failure-rate distribution of new fluid-handling hardware.

parameters.name: K-factor (induced-failure multiplier on intrinsic failure rate), median across ECLSS ORUs
parameters.source: Chen, ICES-2025-127, Appendix Tables 2-11 (ISS MADS-derived; e.g., WPA pump/separator 3.26, avionics air assy 3.2, most items 1.0-1.4)
parameters.notes: Annual failure rate = duty_cycle x quantity x K x 8760 / MTBF. Captures crew error, environment, false alarms, cross-system damage.

parameters.name: Default lognormal error factor (p95/p50) on failure-rate estimates for new hardware
parameters.source: Estimate: EF definition per Anderson et al. AIAA 2012-5320; magnitude anchored to Jones ICES-2019-14 flight ratios (1.9-22x) and Stromgren 2016 MTBF-change distribution
parameters.notes: sigma_ln = ln(EF)/1.645. Use EF~2 for ISS-heritage mature hardware, 5-10 for new-design fluid handling on first Mars deployment.

parameters.name: Accumulated operating time (multiple of MTBF) needed for 95% confidence in MTBF estimate
parameters.source: Stromgren et al., AIAA 2016-5307 (3x for 95% confidence, ~5x for 99%)
parameters.notes: Drives value of pre-deployment testing and lunar/ISS heritage in reducing EF.

parameters.name: UPA Fluids Control & Pump Assembly MTBF, flight-derived
parameters.source: Jones, ICES-2019-14, Table 3 (flight data; MADS pre-flight estimate was 22,759 h). ICES-2025-127 MADS now lists 22,759 h post-redesign
parameters.notes: Worst ECLSS actor: alone drove UPA insufficient-spares probability to 0.983 over 450 d. 1,000 h ~= 41 sols continuous.

parameters.name: UPA Distillation Assembly MTBF, flight-derived
parameters.source: Jones, ICES-2019-14, Table 3 (MADS estimate 41,376 h; CaSO4 precipitation from microgravity bone-loss urine chemistry was root cause)
parameters.notes: Classic unknown-unknown CCF example. PCPA 3,000 h, SPA 4,000 h from same table.

parameters.name: OGA recirculation Pump ORU MTBF, flight-derived
parameters.source: Jones, ICES-2019-14, Table 2 (MADS estimate 189,433 h -> flight 35,000 h, 5.4x worse)
parameters.notes: OGA cell-stack degradation (design-oversight CCF chain) dominated OGA ops impact; cured by ACTEX deionizing bed with ~11,000 h replacement interval.

parameters.name: CDRA valves/heaters effective MTBF (problem components, early flight experience)
parameters.source: Review: 'Toward sustainable living in space' (2025), summarizing ISS heritage: CDRA valves and heaters 800-1,500 h; sorbent dust/check-valve damage per Jones ICES-2019-14
parameters.notes: Post-redesign 2025 MADS lists CDRA selector valve 117,000 h, blower/precooler 129,700 h - use pair (early vs mature) to bracket new-deployment risk.

parameters.name: Median MTBF across mature ISS ECLSS ORUs (2025 MADS)
parameters.source: Chen, Rivas & Rangel, ICES-2025-127, Appendix Tables 2-11 (full ORU population; ~1.3e5 h if restricted to active process ORUs with MTBF <= 1e6 h)
parameters.notes: Median ~1e5 h ~= 4,055 sols. Pumps/rotating machinery cluster at 5,000-20,000 h per 2025 review. verified correction: prior 1e5 h median holds only for the active/process-ORU subset; across the full listed ORU population (which the 8,180-2e8 h range implies) the median is ~1.8e5 h. Min (MCA 8,180 h) and max (passive vents 2e8 h) endpoints are correct.

parameters.name: Median crew time to remove & replace a major ECLSS ORU (excl. gather/stow)
parameters.source: Chen, ICES-2025-127, Table 1 (R&R times: most 2-5 h; OGA hydrogen dome 14 h; WPA gas separator group 19.25 h) and MTTR columns (0.3-17.7 h)
parameters.notes: Add gather/stow + diagnosis + Mars ground-loop (2x ~22 min light time + deliberation) overhead of ~0.5-1.5 h per event.

parameters.name: Target probability of sufficiency (spares) per critical system, Mars mission
parameters.source: EMC practice: Stromgren AIAA 2016-5307 (0.99 DSH reliability spares case), Owens/NASA POS framing ICES-2024-95; ISS historical POS/confidence targets 85-99.5% (Anderson AIAA 2012-5320)
parameters.notes: With ~150 repairable item types, per-item POS must be ~0.9999 (0.99^(1/150)). Crew-survival-critical functions should target 0.999+ with dissimilar backup.

parameters.name: ISS spares inventory maintained on orbit (USOS)
parameters.source: Owens & de Weck, AIAA 2016-5394 (>13,000 kg on orbit; ~18,000 kg staged on ground; >95% of corrective spares expected never used)
parameters.notes: Range max = total staged posture incl. ground. Demonstrates the LEO-with-resupply baseline a Mars base cannot replicate.

parameters.name: Spares mass for 4-crew, 1,100-day Mars transit habitat at R=0.99 (deterministic MTBFs)
parameters.source: Stromgren et al., AIAA 2016-5307 (EMAT, EMC deep-space habitat critical systems incl. ECLSS, TCS, EPS, C&DH, comm, water recovery)
parameters.notes: THE anchor for 4-6 crew Mars hab sparing. ~2,000 kg per Earth-year, ~10%/yr of a 20 t habitat dry mass.

parameters.name: Spares mass to hold 0.99 reliability at 95th-pct confidence with MTBF epistemic uncertainty
parameters.source: Stromgren et al., AIAA 2016-5307 (>12,000 kg low-uncertainty bound, >17,000 kg high-uncertainty); Owens & de Weck AIAA 2016-5394 (17,232 kg for median P(LoC contrib)=1e-3, +4,124 kg per further 10x risk reduction)
parameters.notes: Uncertainty roughly doubles-to-triples the deterministic spares mass. This is the planning value for first Mars missions.

parameters.name: Mars-planning annual spares fraction of supported dry mass (POS 0.99, no resupply)
parameters.source: Derived: 5,984 kg (deterministic) to 17,232 kg (with uncertainty) per AIAA 2016-5307/5394, over 1,100 d against ~20,000 kg habitat dry mass (ICES-2015-288 typical)
parameters.notes: L0 scalar: 0.10/yr; 0.05/yr is the Salyut/Mir/ISS historical heuristic (Larson & Pranke via ICES-2015-288, valid only with resupply); 0.20-0.28 conservative for new hardware at high confidence. Multiply by 1.881 for per-Mars-year rates.

parameters.name: Spares as fraction of all Earth-delivered mass, growing settlement at month 130
parameters.source: Do et al., Acta Astronautica 120 (2016) (Mars One assessment, ISS-derived failure rates, crews added every 26 months)
parameters.notes: Spares come to dominate settlement logistics; range reflects sensitivity to commonality/shared-spares (Jones ICES-2015-044: sharing could cut required spares ~50%).

parameters.name: Reduction in required spares count from parts commonality/reconfigurability across elements
parameters.source: Siddiqi & de Weck, J. Spacecraft & Rockets 44(1) 2007 (Mars surface mission case study; quoted in Jones ICES-2015-044)
parameters.notes: Same availability with 33-50% fewer spares. Caveat: commonality concentrates common-cause/design-defect exposure for unproven designs (Jones ICES-2017-83).

parameters.name: Fraction of a failed ORU's function recoverable via cannibalization/sub-ORU repair
parameters.source: Estimate: ORU-level replacement discards items with useful life (AIAA 2017-5124); shared/cold-spare pooling across co-located elements halves spares in Jones ICES-2015-044 worked example; no flight-quantified value
parameters.notes: Model as probability a corrective demand can be met from the failed-unit boneyard instead of stores; requires sub-ORU repair capability (tools, diagnostics, crew skill).

parameters.name: Fraction of MEL line items manufacturable on demand by near-term ISM (fluid-flow class)
parameters.source: Owens & de Weck, AIAA 2016-5394 (plumbing, ducting, fans, heat exchangers, tanks, valves ~33% of Master Equipment List; 50% used as growth sensitivity)
parameters.notes: Feasibility limited by production rate (AM: 0.01-10 kg/h) vs component time-to-effect (Moraguez & de Weck ICES-2020).

parameters.name: Maintenance-logistics mass reduction from ISM at fixed risk (33% item coverage)
parameters.source: Owens & de Weck, AIAA 2016-5394 (17,232 -> 12,323 kg at P(LoC contrib)=1e-3; 0.34 with feedstock recycling -> 11,254 kg; covered items alone: -78.3%, -97.7% with recycling)
parameters.notes: Also reduces sensitivity to MTBF uncertainty (median P(LoC) contrib 0.027 -> 0.0167 at 33%, 0.0084 at 50% coverage). Feedstock packaging overhead 0.015% vs 1.5% for spares.

parameters.name: Weibull shape parameter, pumps/fans/blowers/compressors (wear-out class)
parameters.source: Estimate: generic rotating-machinery reliability practice (Ebeling, Introduction to Reliability & Maintainability Engineering; OREDA-class industrial data); consistent with ISS pump wear-out history
parameters.notes: Scale eta set so distribution mean matches MTBF/K. ISS program itself assumes exponential (CFR) - use beta=1 at L0/L1, Weibull at L2.

parameters.name: Weibull shape parameter, electronics/controllers/sensors (random-failure class)
parameters.source: Chen, ICES-2025-127 (ISS program assumes constant failure rate / exponential, memoryless)
parameters.notes: Exponential inter-failure times; radiation environment on Mars transit may add an SEU-driven K-factor rather than shape change.

parameters.name: Weibull shape parameter, valves/actuators/mechanisms
parameters.source: Estimate: mixed random/wear behavior; CDRA check-valve dust-abrasion wear (Jones ICES-2019-14) supports beta>1 for particulate-exposed valves
parameters.notes: For Mars dust-exposed mechanisms (airlocks, EVA hardware) bias toward 1.5-2.0 and raise K during dust season (sensitivity parameter, no flight basis).

parameters.name: Weibull shape parameter, infant-mortality phase for newly installed/manufactured units
parameters.source: Estimate: bathtub-curve early phase (Ebeling; simplified bathtub in ICES-2025-127); 'first model year' effect discussed in Stromgren AIAA 2016-5307
parameters.notes: Apply for first ~500-1,000 operating hours of new-design or ISM-printed units, then transition to class shape.

parameters.name: Common-cause failure beta factor across identical redundant units/spares
parameters.source: Estimate: generic PRA beta-factor values (NUREG/CR-5485 class); documented ECLSS CCF chains (OGA cell stack, CDRA dust, UPA urine chemistry) per Jones ICES-2019-14
parameters.notes: Fraction of failures that defeat identical spares/redundancy. This is the term POS math ignores; dissimilar redundancy is the mitigation.

modeling_notes: Core demand model (NASA-standard): per-ORU annual failure rate lambda = duty_cycle x quantity x K_factor x 8760/MTBF (ICES-2025-127); failures over window T are Poisson(lambda*T) with cold-spare POS(n) = sum_{x=0..n} (lambda T)^x e^{-lambda T}/x!; system POS is the product over item types, so per-item POS must be ~POS_sys^(1/N) (~0.9999 for 0.99 over 150 items). Carry epistemic uncertainty by drawing lambda from a lognormal with error factor EF = p95/p50 (sigma_ln = ln(EF)/1.645; gamma-Poisson/negative-binomial equivalent per ICES-2024-95): EF~2 for ISS-heritage hardware, 5-10 for new fluid-handling designs; this uncertainty doubles-to-triples spares mass at fixed confidence (12-17 t vs 6 t for a 4-crew/1,100-d transit hab). Allocate spares by greedy marginal delta-POS/delta-kg (EMAT logic). L0: spares upmass = 10% of supported dry mass per Earth-year (5% optimistic, 28% conservative), maintenance crew time 6.0 h/CM-week, availability 0.99. L1: per-subsystem NB demand + POS optimization + crew-time POS check against work-opportunity time (flag weeks where maintenance demand exceeds available hours). L2: per-ORU renewal processes - exponential for electronics (ISS CFR practice), Weibull beta~1.8 rotating machinery, beta~1.3 valves, deterministic life/throughput clocks for beds/filters/bladders, infant-mortality beta~0.8 for new units; beta-factor CCF ~0.05 across identical units; cannibalization queue (p~0.3 recovery); ISM job shop covering ~33% of items at 0.01-10 kg/h cutting maintenance mass ~28-34%; commonality cuts spares count 33-50%. Crew repair events: R&R time median 3 h (1-19 h) + gather/stow + Mars ground-loop overhead ~0.5-1.5 h. Assumptions to stress-test: dormant systems don't fail; repairs restore good-as-new; failures independent. POS is an upper bound on reliability - CCF and unknown-unknowns historically dominate (OGA cell stack, CDRA dust, UPA CaSO4). Convert MTBF hours to sols by /24.6597; multiply per-Earth-year rates by 1.881 for per-Mars-year (668.6 sols).
key_findings: ISS crews average 6.0 h/crewmember-week of scheduled maintenance (OPTimIS flight data 2017-2023); ECLSS alone consumes 13-15 h/week station-wide versus ~1 h/week predicted at design - an order-of-magnitude underestimate that must be carried into Mars crew-time budgets (~15% of work time).

Failure-rate prediction error dominates sparing risk: 14 of 20 ISS ECLSS ORUs ran worse than predicted, with four UPA ORUs at 7.5-22x the estimated failure rate (FCPA pump flight MTBF ~1,000 h vs 22,759 h predicted); the originally planned spares set would have had a 99.5% chance of running out on a 450-day Mars transit.

Station-wide, Bayesian MADS updates show ~85% of components got better and 15% worse - but the worse tail (3% at <=0.25x MTBF) drives system risk because sparing is asymmetric; model this with a lognormal error factor (EF~2 heritage, 5-10 new designs) feeding gamma-Poisson demand.

Anchor spares masses for a 4-crew, ~1,100-day Mars transit habitat: 5,984 kg at R=0.99 with perfect MTBF knowledge, rising to 12,000-17,232 kg once epistemic uncertainty is included - i.e., ~10%/yr of supported dry mass (vs the 5%/yr Salyut/Mir/ISS heuristic), up to ~28%/yr conservative.

At settlement scale spares dominate logistics: 62% of all Earth-delivered mass by month 130 (Mars One assessment with ISS failure rates); the levers are commonality (33-50% fewer spares), in-space manufacturing (28-34% maintenance-mass reduction at 33% item coverage; -78% on covered items), shared spares pools (~50%), and sub-ORU repair.

NASA sparing math is Poisson demand -> probability of sufficiency, with system POS the product across ~150 item types, so per-item POS ~0.9999 is needed for 0.99 system POS; ISS used POS/confidence targets of 85-99.5%, while no-resupply Mars missions need >=0.99 at high confidence per critical system plus dissimilar backup for crew-survival functions.

For the stochastic failure engine: exponential inter-failure times for electronics (ISS CFR practice), Weibull beta~1.8 (1.2-3.0) for pumps/rotating machinery, beta~1.3 for valves, deterministic life-limit clocks for beds/filters/softgoods, K-factor 1.0-3.3 (median 1.2) for induced failures, beta-factor ~0.05 common-cause overlay, and infant-mortality beta~0.8 for newly installed or 3D-printed units.

K-factors and MTTRs are now published per-ORU (ICES-2025-127, 2025 MADS extract): median ECLSS ORU MTBF ~1e5 h, MTTR median ~1.5-3 h (R&R 1-19 h incl. access), giving hardware availability >=0.999 when spares exist - on Mars, stock-out probability and crew-time contention, not repair duration, drive availability.
